## Entry 77
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T08:57:49.028Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T08:57:49.028Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "custom_tool_call",
    "status": "completed",
    "call_id": "call_mHRvDApXBOlCdW8p7c9FB9XO",
    "name": "apply_patch",
    "input": "*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: AGENTS.md\n+# AGENTS.md\n+\n+## Project Identity\n+- This repository is currently a full-stack scaffold with a working FastAPI backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL setup, Docker configuration, and CI checks.\n+- The intended product direction is `MetroSense`: a Bengaluru-focused climate intelligence assistant that helps planners, disaster-response teams, and logistics operators assess weather and infrastructure risk through a chat interface.\n+- When extending the product, treat the current scaffold as the baseline and evolve it toward MetroSense instead of building parallel app structures.\n+\n+## Tech Stack\n+- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`.\n+- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `npm`.\n+- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy in strict mode, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions.\n+- Infra defaults:\n+  - Local backend runs on `:8010` in README examples.\n+  - Docker Compose exposes API on `:8000`, client on `:5173`, Postgres on `:5433`.\n+\n+## Repository Shape\n+- `server/`\n+  - `app/api/`: HTTP routes, router wiring, FastAPI dependencies.\n+  - `app/services/`: business logic and domain workflows.\n+  - `app/db/`: database models, base metadata, session management.\n+  - `app/core/`: config and logging setup.\n+  - `app/middleware/`: request logging and error handling.\n+  - `tests/unit/`: fast isolated tests for helpers and route behavior.\n+  - `tests/integration/`: app + database level tests.\n+- `client/`\n+  - `src/`: app shell, route rendering, frontend entrypoints, API client utilities.\n+- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n+  - Product/UI direction for the MetroSense chat experience. Use this as the source of truth when implementing the chat interface, risk cards, and artifacts.\n+\n+## Architectural Rules\n+- Preserve backend layering:\n+  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`.\n+  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`.\n+  - `app.db` should not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`.\n+- Keep changes incremental and repo-native:\n+  - Prefer extending existing FastAPI and React entrypoints over introducing new frameworks or duplicate service layers.\n+  - Reuse the current `server/` and `client/` structure unless a structural change is clearly necessary.\n+- Align naming with product intent:\n+  - New user-facing copy, docs, and features should move toward `MetroSense` terminology rather than generic scaffold wording.\n+  - If renaming existing scaffold labels, keep code, docs, and config consistent in the same change.\n+\n+## Product Direction\n+- MetroSense should become a chat-first application where users ask natural-language questions about climate risk, rainfall, flooding, AQI, outages, and infrastructure readiness in Bengaluru.\n+- Expected response modes:\n+  - Streamed assistant text for most interactions.\n+  - Structured `RiskCard` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits risk score data.\n+  - Structured `Artifact` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits chart, table, map, or visualization payloads.\n+- The frontend should not infer structured outputs from keywords; rendering should be driven by explicit backend event or payload types defined by the product spec.\n+\n+## Testing And Validation\n+- Backend checks from `server/`:\n+  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n+  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n+  - `uv run ruff check .`\n+  - `uv run mypy .`\n+  - `uv run lint-imports`\n+  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n+  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n+- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n+  - `npm ci`\n+  - `npm run lint`\n+  - `npm run build`\n+- Local startup:\n+  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n+  - Backend: `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n+  - Frontend: `cd client && npm install && npm run dev`\n+\n+## Current Test Coverage\n+- Backend unit tests currently cover:\n+  - health endpoint behavior\n+  - password/auth-related utility behavior\n+- Backend integration tests currently cover:\n+  - health endpoint integration\n+  - auth flow scaffolding\n+- When adding MetroSense features, add or update tests for:\n+  - API contracts and validation\n+  - service-layer logic\n+  - database interactions where relevant\n+  - frontend state/rendering for any new structured chat response types\n+\n+## Agent Guidance\n+- Before major implementation work, confirm repo reality from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n+- Prefer `rg` for codebase search.\n+- Do not invent unimplemented services or folders in docs or code without creating them deliberately.\n+- If a feature conflicts with the existing scaffold and the MetroSense spec, treat the spec as the intended direction, but keep the implementation grounded in the current codebase and note any transition gaps clearly.\n*** End Patch\n"
  }
}
```
**Metadata:**
```json
{
  "payload": {
    "type": "custom_tool_call",
    "status": "completed",
    "call_id": "call_mHRvDApXBOlCdW8p7c9FB9XO",
    "name": "apply_patch",
    "input": "*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: AGENTS.md\n+# AGENTS.md\n+\n+## Project Identity\n+- This repository is currently a full-stack scaffold with a working FastAPI backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL setup, Docker configuration, and CI checks.\n+- The intended product direction is `MetroSense`: a Bengaluru-focused climate intelligence assistant that helps planners, disaster-response teams, and logistics operators assess weather and infrastructure risk through a chat interface.\n+- When extending the product, treat the current scaffold as the baseline and evolve it toward MetroSense instead of building parallel app structures.\n+\n+## Tech Stack\n+- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`.\n+- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `npm`.\n+- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy in strict mode, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions.\n+- Infra defaults:\n+  - Local backend runs on `:8010` in README examples.\n+  - Docker Compose exposes API on `:8000`, client on `:5173`, Postgres on `:5433`.\n+\n+## Repository Shape\n+- `server/`\n+  - `app/api/`: HTTP routes, router wiring, FastAPI dependencies.\n+  - `app/services/`: business logic and domain workflows.\n+  - `app/db/`: database models, base metadata, session management.\n+  - `app/core/`: config and logging setup.\n+  - `app/middleware/`: request logging and error handling.\n+  - `tests/unit/`: fast isolated tests for helpers and route behavior.\n+  - `tests/integration/`: app + database level tests.\n+- `client/`\n+  - `src/`: app shell, route rendering, frontend entrypoints, API client utilities.\n+- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n+  - Product/UI direction for the MetroSense chat experience. Use this as the source of truth when implementing the chat interface, risk cards, and artifacts.\n+\n+## Architectural Rules\n+- Preserve backend layering:\n+  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`.\n+  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`.\n+  - `app.db` should not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`.\n+- Keep changes incremental and repo-native:\n+  - Prefer extending existing FastAPI and React entrypoints over introducing new frameworks or duplicate service layers.\n+  - Reuse the current `server/` and `client/` structure unless a structural change is clearly necessary.\n+- Align naming with product intent:\n+  - New user-facing copy, docs, and features should move toward `MetroSense` terminology rather than generic scaffold wording.\n+  - If renaming existing scaffold labels, keep code, docs, and config consistent in the same change.\n+\n+## Product Direction\n+- MetroSense should become a chat-first application where users ask natural-language questions about climate risk, rainfall, flooding, AQI, outages, and infrastructure readiness in Bengaluru.\n+- Expected response modes:\n+  - Streamed assistant text for most interactions.\n+  - Structured `RiskCard` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits risk score data.\n+  - Structured `Artifact` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits chart, table, map, or visualization payloads.\n+- The frontend should not infer structured outputs from keywords; rendering should be driven by explicit backend event or payload types defined by the product spec.\n+\n+## Testing And Validation\n+- Backend checks from `server/`:\n+  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n+  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n+  - `uv run ruff check .`\n+  - `uv run mypy .`\n+  - `uv run lint-imports`\n+  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n+  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n+- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n+  - `npm ci`\n+  - `npm run lint`\n+  - `npm run build`\n+- Local startup:\n+  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n+  - Backend: `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n+  - Frontend: `cd client && npm install && npm run dev`\n+\n+## Current Test Coverage\n+- Backend unit tests currently cover:\n+  - health endpoint behavior\n+  - password/auth-related utility behavior\n+- Backend integration tests currently cover:\n+  - health endpoint integration\n+  - auth flow scaffolding\n+- When adding MetroSense features, add or update tests for:\n+  - API contracts and validation\n+  - service-layer logic\n+  - database interactions where relevant\n+  - frontend state/rendering for any new structured chat response types\n+\n+## Agent Guidance\n+- Before major implementation work, confirm repo reality from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n+- Prefer `rg` for codebase search.\n+- Do not invent unimplemented services or folders in docs or code without creating them deliberately.\n+- If a feature conflicts with the existing scaffold and the MetroSense spec, treat the spec as the intended direction, but keep the implementation grounded in the current codebase and note any transition gaps clearly.\n*** End Patch\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 83
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T08:57:55.897Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T08:57:55.897Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_0nqBwHkiyfp9SHaVzMCCzwA0",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 1f2f70\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1189\nOutput:\n# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is currently a full-stack scaffold with a working FastAPI backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL setup, Docker configuration, and CI checks.\n- The intended product direction is `MetroSense`: a Bengaluru-focused climate intelligence assistant that helps planners, disaster-response teams, and logistics operators assess weather and infrastructure risk through a chat interface.\n- When extending the product, treat the current scaffold as the baseline and evolve it toward MetroSense instead of building parallel app structures.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`.\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `npm`.\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy in strict mode, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions.\n- Infra defaults:\n  - Local backend runs on `:8010` in README examples.\n  - Docker Compose exposes API on `:8000`, client on `:5173`, Postgres on `:5433`.\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `server/`\n  - `app/api/`: HTTP routes, router wiring, FastAPI dependencies.\n  - `app/services/`: business logic and domain workflows.\n  - `app/db/`: database models, base metadata, session management.\n  - `app/core/`: config and logging setup.\n  - `app/middleware/`: request logging and error handling.\n  - `tests/unit/`: fast isolated tests for helpers and route behavior.\n  - `tests/integration/`: app + database level tests.\n- `client/`\n  - `src/`: app shell, route rendering, frontend entrypoints, API client utilities.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Product/UI direction for the MetroSense chat experience. Use this as the source of truth when implementing the chat interface, risk cards, and artifacts.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`.\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`.\n  - `app.db` should not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`.\n- Keep changes incremental and repo-native:\n  - Prefer extending existing FastAPI and React entrypoints over introducing new frameworks or duplicate service layers.\n  - Reuse the current `server/` and `client/` structure unless a structural change is clearly necessary.\n- Align naming with product intent:\n  - New user-facing copy, docs, and features should move toward `MetroSense` terminology rather than generic scaffold wording.\n  - If renaming existing scaffold labels, keep code, docs, and config consistent in the same change.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense should become a chat-first application where users ask natural-language questions about climate risk, rainfall, flooding, AQI, outages, and infrastructure readiness in Bengaluru.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - Streamed assistant text for most interactions.\n  - Structured `RiskCard` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits risk score data.\n  - Structured `Artifact` responses when the backend/agent explicitly emits chart, table, map, or visualization payloads.\n- The frontend should not infer structured outputs from keywords; rendering should be driven by explicit backend event or payload types defined by the product spec.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `npm ci`\n  - `npm run lint`\n  - `npm run build`\n- Local startup:\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - Backend: `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - Frontend: `cd client && npm install && npm run dev`\n\n## Current Test Coverage\n- Backend unit tests currently cover:\n  - health endpoint behavior\n  - password/auth-related utility behavior\n- Backend integration tests currently cover:\n  - health endpoint integration\n  - auth flow scaffolding\n- When adding MetroSense features, add or update tests for:\n  - API contracts and validation\n  - service-layer logic\n  - database interactions where relevant\n  - frontend state/rendering for any new structured chat response types\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, confirm repo reality from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for codebase search.\n- Do not invent unimplemented services or folders in docs or code without creating them deliberately.\n- If a feature conflicts with the existing scaffold and the MetroSense spec, treat the spec as the intended direction, but keep the implementation grounded in the current codebase and note any transition gaps clearly.\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 90
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:00:59.736Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:00:59.736Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "developer",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "input_text",
        "text": "<collaboration_mode># Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n</collaboration_mode>"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

(file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",

Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
```json
      }
```
```json
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

```json
    "call_id": "call_apaAbB6xPeJvtYYVsrhweqe8",
    "output": "Chunk ID: ad12db\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 2662\nOutput:\nclient/vite.config.ts:9:      \"/api\": \"http://localhost:8010\",\nclient/vite.config.ts:10:      \"/health\": \"http://localhost:8010\",\nREADME.md:60:uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010\nREADME.md:68:App runs at http://localhost:5173 with API proxied to :8010.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:1:# FRONTEND_SPEC.md — MetroSense Chat Interface\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:5:A minimal, single-page chat interface for the **UrbanClimate AI** system, serving city planners, BBMP disaster teams, and logistics companies in evaluating weather-related risks and infrastructure readiness for Bengaluru.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:7:Users interact via natural language. The agent queries a local PostgreSQL database (real-time sensor data: temperature, humidity, wind speed, rainfall, AQI, lake water levels, power outage logs, traffic heatmaps) and retrieves context from local documentation (IMD weather bulletins, BBMP drainage master plans, flood post-mortem reports, urban forest maps, climate resilience whitepapers). The UI is a **plain chat interface** — it shows streamed text responses for the vast majority of queries.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:9:The chat interface has **two structured output types** beyond plain streamed text — both are agent-decided and never client-inferred:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:11:1. **RiskCard** — rendered when the agent emits a `risk_card` SSE event carrying a neighborhood risk scorecard as structured JSON (flood probability, power outage risk, traffic delay index, health advisory, emergency readiness).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:12:2. **Artifact** — rendered when the agent emits an `artifact` SSE event carrying a self-contained HTML or JSX string representing a chart, graph, table, or data visualisation. The frontend parses and sandboxes this markup and renders it inline in the conversation. The agent produces these when a visual answer (e.g. a rainfall trend chart, an AQI heatmap, a congestion timeline) is more useful than prose.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:25:    │  HTTP POST /api/chat  (message + session_id)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:27:FastAPI Backend  (server/)          ← port 8010\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:31:Agent Server  (agent/)              ← same repo, same Docker Compose\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:38:The Agent Server is a **first-party process you own** — running in the same Docker Compose stack. The FastAPI backend calls it **in-process** (direct Python import) or over localhost if you choose to run it as a separate uvicorn process on a different port (e.g. `:8020`). Either way, the frontend never calls it directly — all chat traffic goes through `POST /api/chat` on the FastAPI backend.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:46:| `/` | `ChatPage` | The only page. Full-height chat interface. |\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:59:    │   ├── StatusPill (agent connection status)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:64:    │   │   ├── RiskCard?         (optional — agent risk_card SSE event)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:65:    │   │   └── ArtifactRenderer? (optional — agent artifact SSE event)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:111:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the agent emits a `risk_card` SSE event.** This is the structured output for a neighborhood risk scorecard. It is never shown for plain conversational responses — not for flood questions, not for AQI questions, not for any keyword match. The agent decides when a scorecard is warranted.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:113:The card surfaces four risk dimensions derived from the agent's cross-analysis of sensor data and documentation:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:142:- `flood_risk` — agent's cross-reference of predicted rainfall intensity against drainage capacity documents (BBMP master plan, 2022 flood post-mortems) and lake water level telemetry.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:146:- `emergency_readiness` — agent's final recommendation and specific action items (e.g., underpass barricading, evacuation routes).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:150:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the agent emits an `artifact` SSE event.** The agent produces artifacts when a chart, graph, table, or data visualisation communicates the answer better than prose — for example, a 7-day rainfall trend chart, a neighbourhood AQI heatmap, or a congestion timeline by hour.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:152:#### 5.5.1 What the agent sends\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:154:The `artifact` SSE payload carries a self-contained string of either HTML or JSX. The agent sets a `type` field to indicate which:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:165:Examples of artifacts the agent might produce:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:176:1. Receive the raw HTML string from the agent.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:177:2. Sanitise with DOMPurify (strip `<script>`, event handlers, external resource URLs) — never trust agent-produced markup directly.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:222:Shown while awaiting agent response.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:255:### POST `/api/chat`\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:280:- `risk_card` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `riskCard` and render a RiskCard below the streamed text. The frontend must never emit or synthesize this event itself. A single response can contain both `token` events and one `risk_card` event. The `risk_card` event may arrive before, during, or after the token stream — the frontend must handle all orderings gracefully.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:281:- `artifact` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `artifact` and render an `ArtifactRenderer` below any RiskCard. Payload schema: `{ type: \"html\" | \"jsx\", title: string, source: string, description?: string }`. The frontend must sanitise/sandbox the content before rendering (see §5.5). A response may contain at most one `artifact` event; if multiple arrive, render only the last one and log a warning.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:285:**GET `/api/health`** — used by StatusPill to poll agent connectivity (poll every 30s).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:291:The agent emits this as a `risk_card` SSE event payload when it determines a structured neighborhood scorecard is the appropriate response. The frontend renders it as a RiskCard component. All top-level metric fields are optional — the agent only populates fields it has sufficient data to compute.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:296:  generated_at: string;           // ISO 8601 — timestamp of agent analysis\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:342:const [agentStatus, setAgentStatus] = useState<'online' | 'connecting' | 'error'>('connecting');\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:351:  source: string;              // raw HTML or JSX string from the agent\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:379:│   ├── useChat.ts                 # All chat logic: send, stream, abort, session\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:384:│   ├── chat/\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:396:│   └── chat.ts                    # Message, RiskCardPayload, ArtifactPayload interfaces\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:491:| `AGENT_SERVER_URL` | `http://agent:8020` | Internal Agent Server URL (Docker Compose service name). Only used if agent runs as a separate process; omit if in-process. |\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:492:| `VITE_API_BASE_URL` | *(proxied via Vite → :8010)* | Frontend API base (already handled by Vite proxy in scaffold) |\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:501:server/app/api/routes/chat.py\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:505:- Accept `POST /api/chat` with `{ session_id, message }`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:506:- Call the Agent Server via `agent_proxy.stream_chat()`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:508:- Accept `DELETE /api/chat/{session_id}` to clear agent session state.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:513:server/app/services/agent_proxy.py\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:517:- `async def stream_chat(session_id, message) → AsyncIterator[str]`\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:519:- **If Agent Server runs as a separate process on localhost:** call `http://localhost:8020/chat` (internal only, never crosses the internet).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:525:agent:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:526:  build: ./agent          # your Agent Server directory\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:528:    - \"8020:8020\"         # internal only; not exposed if in-process\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:540:- Chat history persistence (no DB writes for chat messages).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:543:- Multi-agent routing or tool call visibility in the UI.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:544:- **Keyword-based or client-side RiskCard triggering.** The frontend must never inspect message content to decide whether to show a RiskCard. That decision belongs entirely to the agent.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:545:- **Artifact execution beyond the approved library whitelist.** The JSX artifact parser exposes only `React`, `ReactDOM`, `recharts`, `victory`, and `d3`. Any artifact requiring other libraries (e.g. Mapbox, Leaflet, Plotly) is out of scope for the inline renderer — the agent should not produce such artifacts. If it does, `ArtifactRenderer` must show the error state, not attempt execution.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:546:- **Agent-side artifact generation logic.** This spec covers only parsing and rendering on the frontend. How the agent decides to produce an artifact (and what chart library it uses) is defined in the agent spec, not here.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:547:- **Multi-artifact messages.** A single agent response renders at most one artifact. If the agent sends multiple `artifact` events in one response, only the last is rendered.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:555:3. `types/chat.ts` — lock the interfaces including `ArtifactPayload`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:565:13. `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` + `agent_proxy.py` — real backend integration.\nserver/uv.lock:517:    { name = \"google-cloud-aiplatform\", extra = [\"agent-engines\"] },\nserver/uv.lock:657:agent-engines = [\nserver/uv.lock:1052:    { url = \"https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/5c/c5/cc09412a29e43406eba18d61c70baa936e299bc27e074e2be3806ed29098/greenlet-3.3.2-cp312-cp312-manylinux_2_24_s390x.manylinux_2_28_s390x.whl\", hash = \"sha256:ae9e21c84035c490506c17002f5c8ab25f980205c3e61ddb3a2a2a2e6c411fcb\", size = 626250, upload-time = \"2026-02-20T21:02:46.596Z\" },\nserver/uv.lock:2228:    { url = \"https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/f9/11/ba845c23988798f40e52ba45f34849aa8a1f2d4af4b798588010792ebad6/pyyaml-6.0.3-cp313-cp313-musllinux_1_2_aarch64.whl\", hash = \"sha256:f7057c9a337546edc7973c0d3ba84ddcdf0daa14533c2065749c9075001090e6\", size = 753613, upload-time = \"2025-09-25T21:32:30.178Z\" },\nserver/uv.lock:2279:    { url = \"https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/1e/db/4254e3eabe8020b458f1a747140d32277ec7a271daf1d235b70dc0b4e6e3/requests-2.32.5-py3-none-any.whl\", hash = \"sha256:2462f94637a34fd532264295e186976db0f5d453d1cdd31473c85a6a161affb6\", size = 64738, upload-time = \"2025-08-18T20:46:00.542Z\" },\n"
  }
}
```

```json
    "call_id": "call_kP7jWxpGCBNyx0nfM6GfIhWy"
  }
}
```

## Entry 103
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:02:08.062Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:02:08.062Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "item_completed",
    "thread_id": "019cd6ee-26e3-7322-8be2-9f5ebbc341b9",
    "turn_id": "019cd6fa-63d6-7313-b247-7a1f0a109c22",
    "item": {
      "type": "Plan",
      "id": "019cd6fa-63d6-7313-b247-7a1f0a109c22-plan",
      "text": "# Revise `AGENTS.md` for Three-Service MetroSense Architecture\n\n## Summary\nUpdate the root `AGENTS.md` so it documents both the current scaffold and the intended target architecture: `client/` frontend, `server/` FastAPI backend, and a new `agents/` service that runs its own server. The canonical interaction model should be `frontend -> backend -> agent`, with the frontend never calling the agent service directly.\n\n## Key Changes\n- Rewrite the project identity section to reflect two truths clearly:\n  - Current implementation: scaffolded FastAPI + React + PostgreSQL repo.\n  - Target architecture: MetroSense chat platform with three coordinated services, where `agents/` hosts the chat/agent server.\n- Update the repository structure section to include planned `agents/` responsibilities:\n  - `client/`: chat UI, streamed rendering, risk/artifact presentation.\n  - `server/`: public API, request validation, session coordination, backend-to-agent orchestration.\n  - `agents/`: agent runtime, tool execution, structured response generation, domain intelligence.\n- Add an architecture section that locks the communication path:\n  - Frontend sends chat traffic only to FastAPI.\n  - FastAPI calls the agent server internally.\n  - Agent server returns streamed text and optional structured payloads back through the backend.\n  - Document that direct frontend-to-agent calls are out of scope for the canonical design.\n- Update product-direction guidance to align with the new split:\n  - Chat service is the primary product surface.\n  - Structured outputs such as risk cards and artifacts are agent-decided, then forwarded by the backend to the frontend.\n  - `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` remains the UI/product source of truth, but any `agent/` references in that doc should be treated as conceptually mapping to the planned `agents/` service until docs are aligned.\n- Add agent-facing implementation rules for future work:\n  - Prefer extending the existing `server/` API rather than bypassing it.\n  - Keep contracts between backend and agent explicit and typed once implemented.\n  - Treat `agents/` as an internal service boundary, not a second public API surface.\n\n## Public Interfaces / Conventions\n- `AGENTS.md` should define these canonical conventions:\n  - Planned folder/service name is `agents/`.\n  - Public client API surface is the FastAPI backend.\n  - Agent service is internal and backend-mediated.\n- The doc should explicitly mark `agents/` as planned architecture if the folder does not yet exist, so the file stays truthful without blocking future work.\n\n## Test Plan\n- Cross-check all doc claims against current repo files:\n  - `README.md`\n  - `server/pyproject.toml`\n  - `client/package.json`\n  - `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n- Verify all existing validation commands in `AGENTS.md` still match repo scripts and tools.\n- Review the final wording for consistency around `agents/` vs `agent/`, ensuring the file explains the current naming mismatch instead of silently contradicting repo docs.\n\n## Assumptions\n- Canonical planned folder name is `agents/`, even though current spec text uses `agent/`.\n- Canonical runtime path is `frontend -> backend -> agent`, with no direct frontend access to the agent server.\n- `AGENTS.md` should document target architecture now, but must clearly label it as planned where the repo has not implemented it yet.\n"
    }
  }
}
```

`README.md`\n  - `server/pyproject.toml`\n  - `client/package.json`\n  - `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n- Verify all existing validation commands in `AGENTS.md` still match repo scripts and tools.\n- Review the final wording for consistency around `agents/` vs `agent/`, ensuring the file explains the current naming mismatch instead of silently contradicting repo docs.\n\n## Assumptions\n- Canonical planned folder name is `agents/`, even though current spec text uses `agent/`.\n- Canonical runtime path is `frontend -> backend -> agent`, with no direct frontend access to the agent server.\n- `AGENTS.md` should document target architecture now, but must clearly label it as planned where the repo has not implemented it yet.\n</proposed_plan>"
```json
      }
    ],
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "output_text",
        "text": "<proposed_plan>\n# Revise `AGENTS.md` for Three-Service MetroSense Architecture\n\n## Summary\nUpdate the root `AGENTS.md` so it documents both the current scaffold and the intended target architecture: `client/` frontend, `server/` FastAPI backend, and a new `agents/` service that runs its own server. The canonical interaction model should be `frontend -> backend -> agent`, with the frontend never calling the agent service directly.\n\n## Key Changes\n- Rewrite the project identity section to reflect two truths clearly:\n  - Current implementation: scaffolded FastAPI + React + PostgreSQL repo.\n  - Target architecture: MetroSense chat platform with three coordinated services, where `agents/` hosts the chat/agent server.\n- Update the repository structure section to include planned `agents/` responsibilities:\n  - `client/`: chat UI, streamed rendering, risk/artifact presentation.\n  - `server/`: public API, request validation, session coordination, backend-to-agent orchestration.\n  - `agents/`: agent runtime, tool execution, structured response generation, domain intelligence.\n- Add an architecture section that locks the communication path:\n  - Frontend sends chat traffic only to FastAPI.\n  - FastAPI calls the agent server internally.\n  - Agent server returns streamed text and optional structured payloads back through the backend.\n  - Document that direct frontend-to-agent calls are out of scope for the canonical design.\n- Update product-direction guidance to align with the new split:\n  - Chat service is the primary product surface.\n  - Structured outputs such as risk cards and artifacts are agent-decided, then forwarded by the backend to the frontend.\n  - `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` remains the UI/product source of truth, but any `agent/` references in that doc should be treated as conceptually mapping to the planned `agents/` service until docs are aligned.\n- Add agent-facing implementation rules for future work:\n  - Prefer extending the existing `server/` API rather than bypassing it.\n  - Keep contracts between backend and agent explicit and typed once implemented.\n  - Treat `agents/` as an internal service boundary, not a second public API surface.\n\n## Public Interfaces / Conventions\n- `AGENTS.md` should define these canonical conventions:\n  - Planned folder/service name is `agents/`.\n  - Public client API surface is the FastAPI backend.\n  - Agent service is internal and backend-mediated.\n- The doc should explicitly mark `agents/` as planned architecture if the folder does not yet exist, so the file stays truthful without blocking future work.\n\n## Test Plan\n- Cross-check all doc claims against current repo files:\n  - `README.md`\n  - `server/pyproject.toml`\n  - `client/package.json`\n  - `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n- Verify all existing validation commands in `AGENTS.md` still match repo scripts and tools.\n- Review the final wording for consistency around `agents/` vs `agent/`, ensuring the file explains the current naming mismatch instead of silently contradicting repo docs.\n\n## Assumptions\n- Canonical planned folder name is `agents/`, even though current spec text uses `agent/`.\n- Canonical runtime path is `frontend -> backend -> agent`, with no direct frontend access to the agent server.\n- `AGENTS.md` should document target architecture now, but must clearly label it as planned where the repo has not implemented it yet.\n</proposed_plan>"
```

## Entry 108
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:13:02.661Z
**Role/Type:** turn_context
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:13:02.661Z",
  "type": "turn_context",
  "payload": {
    "turn_id": "019cd705-6bc2-7d73-acc5-4ce12c8d230c",
    "cwd": "/home/dell/ai-track-metrosense",
    "current_date": "2026-03-10",
    "timezone": "Asia/Kolkata",
    "approval_policy": "on-request",
    "sandbox_policy": {
      "type": "workspace-write",
      "writable_roots": [
        "/home/dell/.codex/memories"
      ],
      "network_access": false,
      "exclude_tmpdir_env_var": false,
      "exclude_slash_tmp": false
    },
    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "plan",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.4",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
```json
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

## Entry 113
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:13:29.883Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:13:29.883Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "Yes. The main reason is control.\n\nIf the frontend calls the agent directly, you turn the agent into a public-facing service. That usually makes the system harder to secure, evolve, and operate.\n\n**Recommended Flow**\n`frontend -> backend -> agent`\n\n**Why this is usually better**\n\n1. `Security`\n- The backend hides agent credentials, tool credentials, DB access, and internal endpoints.\n- The agent can stay on a private network and not be exposed to the browser.\n- You can enforce auth, rate limits, tenancy, input validation, and audit logging in one place.\n\n2. `Stable public API`\n- The frontend talks to one API you own: the backend.\n- You can change agent frameworks, prompts, toolchains, or even replace the agent entirely without breaking the UI.\n- The backend becomes the contract boundary.\n\n3. `Business control`\n- The backend can decide:\n  - which agent to call\n  - when to call fallback logic\n  - how to shape/clean responses\n  - what to persist\n  - what to redact\n- This is important once chat behavior becomes product logic, not just model output.\n\n4. `Streaming + orchestration`\n- The backend can merge:\n  - agent output\n  - database reads\n  - session state\n  - feature flags\n  - safety checks\n- That is much cleaner than making the browser coordinate multiple internal systems.\n\n5. `Observability`\n- One place for logs, traces, retries, error translation, metrics, and cost tracking.\n- Easier to answer: “why did this chat fail?” or “which agent response caused this UI issue?”\n\n6. `Future-proofing`\n- Later you may add:\n  - multiple agents\n  - non-agent deterministic endpoints\n  - human review steps\n  - caching\n  - billing/quotas\n- Backend mediation makes that much easier.\n\n**Why direct frontend -> agent is tempting**\n\n1. `Less plumbing`\n- Faster to prototype.\n- Fewer layers.\n- Lower initial implementation effort.\n\n2. `Potentially lower latency`\n- One less hop.\n- Sometimes simpler SSE/WebSocket setup.\n\n3. `Good for internal demos`\n- If speed matters more than control, direct calls can be acceptable temporarily.\n\n**Problems with direct frontend -> agent**\n\n1. `Security exposure`\n- The agent becomes internet-facing.\n- Harder to protect tool access and internal capabilities.\n\n2. `Leaky architecture`\n- The frontend now depends on agent-specific request/response shapes.\n- Swapping agent implementations becomes expensive.\n\n3. `Weaker governance`\n- Harder to enforce product rules before the agent runs.\n- Harder to sanitize outputs consistently.\n\n4. `Operational mess`\n- Auth, rate limiting, CORS, logging, retries, and abuse protection now need to exist on the agent service too.\n\n5. `Browser trust problem`\n- The browser is an untrusted client.\n- If the agent has powerful tools, direct exposure is usually the wrong boundary.\n\n**When direct frontend -> agent can be okay**\n- Early prototype\n- Internal-only tool\n- No sensitive tools or data\n- No strict auth/compliance needs\n- You are fine rewriting the integration later\n\n**Practical recommendation for your project**\nFor MetroSense, `frontend -> backend -> agent` is the stronger design because:\n- the agent will likely touch private data/tools\n- the backend should own chat session/API contracts\n- the frontend should stay focused on rendering streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\nA good mental model is:\n- `frontend`: presentation\n- `backend`: policy + contract + orchestration\n- `agent`: intelligence engine\n\nIf you want, I can also give you a concrete decision table for:\n- MVP startup phase\n- beta phase\n- production phase",
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

Backend mediation makes that much easier.\n\n**Why direct frontend -> agent is tempting**\n\n1. `Less plumbing`\n- Faster to prototype.\n- Fewer layers.\n- Lower initial implementation effort.\n\n2. `Potentially lower latency`\n- One less hop.\n- Sometimes simpler SSE/WebSocket setup.\n\n3. `Good for internal demos`\n- If speed matters more than control, direct calls can be acceptable too.\n\n5. `Browser trust problem`\n- The browser is an untrusted client.\n- If the agent has powerful tools, direct exposure is usually the wrong boundary.\n\n**When direct frontend -> agent can be frontend should stay focused on rendering streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\nA good mental model is:\n- `frontend`: presentation\n- `backend`: policy + contract + orchestration\n- `agent`: intelligence engine\n\nIf you want, I can also give you a concrete decision table for:\n- MVP startup phase\n- beta phase\n- production phase",
```json
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```
