These reflect the UrbanClimate AI's core capabilities.\n   240\t\n   241\tFour chips in a 2×2 grid:\n   242\t- \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses should be barricaded?\"\n   243\t- \"How does current humidity compare to pre-monsoon averages of the last decade?\"\n   244\t- \"Which logistics routes through Bengaluru are highest risk today?\"\n   245\t- \"Generate a risk scorecard for Sarjapur Road.\"\n   246\t\n   247\t> **Note:** Only the last prompt reliably triggers a RiskCard — it explicitly requests a structured scorecard. The others are natural language queries that return streamed text. Chips are for user convenience, not a trigger mechanism.\n   248\t\n   249\tClicking a chip populates and submits the textarea.\n   250\t\n   251\t---\n   252\t\n   253\t## 6. API Contract\n   254\t\n   255\t### POST `/api/chat`\n   256\t\n   257\t**Request:**\n   258\t```json\n   259\t{\n   260\t  \"session_id\": \"uuid-v4\",\n   261\t  \"message\": \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses should be barricaded based on previous years?\"\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t**Response (streaming — `text/event-stream` SSE):**\n   266\t```\n   267\tdata: {\"type\": \"token\", \"content\": \"Based on the 2022 flood post-mortem\"}\n   268\tdata: {\"type\": \"token\", \"content\": \" and current drainage capacity...\"}\n   269\tdata: {\"type\": \"risk_card\", \"payload\": { ...structured risk JSON... }}\n   270\tdata: {\"type\": \"done\"}\n   271\t```\n   272\t\n   273\t**Error response:**\n   274\t```json\n   275\t{ \"error\": \"AGENT_UNAVAILABLE\", \"message\": \"...\" }\n   276\t```\n   277\t\n   278\tThe frontend handles five SSE event types:\n   279\t- `token` — append content to the current assistant bubble's text.\n   280\t- `risk_card` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `riskCard` and render a RiskCard below the streamed text. The frontend must never emit or synthesize this event itself. A single response can contain both `token` events and one `risk_card` event. The `risk_card` event may arrive before, during, or after the token stream — the frontend must handle all orderings gracefully.\n   281\t- `artifact` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `artifact` and render an `ArtifactRenderer` below any RiskCard. Payload schema: `{ type: \"html\" | \"jsx\", title: string, source: string, description?: string }`. The frontend must sanitise/sandbox the content before rendering (see §5.5). A response may contain at most one `artifact` event; if multiple arrive, render only the last one and log a warning.\n   282\t- `done` — mark message complete, re-enable input.\n   283\t- `error` — show inline error banner in the message bubble.\n   284\t\n   285\t**GET `/api/health`** — used by StatusPill to poll agent connectivity (poll every 30s).\n   286\t\n   287\t---\n   288\t\n   289\t## 7. Risk Card JSON Schema\n   290\t\n   291\tThe agent emits this as a `risk_card` SSE event payload when it determines a structured neighborhood scorecard is the appropriate response. The frontend renders it as a RiskCard component. All top-level metric fields are optional — the agent only populates fields it has sufficient data to compute.\n   292\t\n   293\t```typescript\n   294\tinterface RiskCardPayload {\n   295\t  neighborhood: string;           // e.g. \"SARJAPUR ROAD\", \"BELLANDUR\", \"MANYATA TECH PARK\"\n   296\t  generated_at: string;           // ISO 8601 — timestamp of agent analysis\n   297\t  overall_risk_score: number;     // 0–10 composite score\n   298\t\n   299\t  // Cross-reference: DB rainfall forecast + drainage capacity docs + lake telemetry\n   300\t  flood_risk?: {\n   301\t    probability: number;          // 0–1\n   302\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   303\t  };\n   304\t\n   305\t  // Cross-reference: wind-speed telemetry + tree-density maps + historical outage logs\n   306\t  power_outage_risk?: {\n   307\t    probability: number;          // 0–1\n   308\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   309\t  };\n   310\t\n   311\t  // Derived from: rainfall threshold / traffic bottleneck historical correlation\n   312\t  traffic_delay_index?: {\n   313\t    congestion_score: number;     // 0–10\n   314\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   315\t  };\n   316\t\n   317\t  // Derived from: AQI sensor data + Urban Heat Island research docs\n   318\t  health_advisory?: {\n   319\t    aqi: number;                  // raw AQI value\n   320\t    aqi_category: string;         // e.g. \"GOOD\", \"MODERATE\", \"UNHEALTHY FOR SENSITIVE GROUPS\", \"UNHEALTHY\", \"HAZARDOUS\"\n   321\t  };\n   322\t\n   323\t  // Agent's final recommendations based on full cross-analysis\n   324\t  emergency_readiness?: {\n   325\t    recommendation: string;       // e.g. \"Evacuate low-lying zones; barricade underpasses\"\n   326\t    actions?: string[];           // Specific action items, e.g. [\"Barricade Silk Board underpass\", \"Pre-position pumps at Bellandur inlet\"]\n   327\t  };\n   328\t}\n   329\t```\n   330\t\n   331\t---\n   332\t\n   333\t## 8. State Management\n   334\t\n   335\tNo external state library needed. Use React `useState` + `useRef`.\n   336\t\n   337\t```typescript\n   338\t// ChatPage state\n   339\tconst [messages, setMessages] = useState<Message[]>([]);\n   340\tconst [input, setInput] = useState('');\n   341\tconst [isStreaming, setIsStreaming] = useState(false);\n   342\tconst [agentStatus, setAgentStatus] = useState<'online' | 'connecting' | 'error'>('connecting');\n   343\tconst sessionId = useRef(crypto.randomUUID());\n   344\tconst abortController = useRef<AbortController | null>(null);\n   345\t```\n   346\t\n   347\t```typescript\n   348\tinterface ArtifactPayload {\n   349\t  type: 'html' | 'jsx';\n   350\t  title: string;\n   351\t  source: string;              // raw HTML or JSX string from the agent\n   352\t  description?: string;        // plain-text alt for screen readers / error fallback\n   353\t}\n   354\t\n   355\tinterface Message {\n   356\t  id: string;\n   357\t  role: 'user' | 'assistant';\n   358\t  content: string;             // accumulated text\n   359\t  riskCard?: RiskCardPayload;  // optional structured block\n   360\t  artifact?: ArtifactPayload;  // optional chart / visualisation artifact\n   361\t  timestamp: Date;\n   362\t  isStreaming?: boolean;\n   363\t}\n   364\t```\n   365\t\n   366\tArtifact parse state (parsing/transpiling is async) is managed separately inside `ArtifactRenderer` — it does not live on `Message` to avoid polluting the message store with transient UI state.\n   367\t\n   368\t---\n   369\t\n   370\t## 9. File Structure\n   371\t\n   372\t```\n   373\tclient/src/\n   374\t├── App.tsx                        # Single route → ChatPage\n   375\t├── main.tsx                       # Providers (ThemeProvider, etc.)\n   376\t├── lib/\n   377\t│   └── api.ts                     # Axios instance (already in scaffold) + SSE helper\n   378\t├── hooks/\n   379\t│   ├── useChat.ts                 # All chat logic: send, stream, abort, session\n   380\t│   └── useArtifactParser.ts       # Parses/transpiles ArtifactPayload → renderable output\n   381\t├── components/\n   382\t│   ├── layout/\n   383\t│   │   └── Header.tsx\n   384\t│   ├── chat/\n   385\t│   │   ├── MessageList.tsx\n   386\t│   │   ├── MessageBubble.tsx\n   387\t│   │   ├── RiskCard.tsx\n   388\t│   │   ├── ArtifactRenderer.tsx   # Sandboxed iframe (HTML) or evaluated JSX renderer\n   389\t│   │   ├── ThinkingIndicator.tsx\n   390\t│   │   └── SuggestedPrompts.tsx\n   391\t│   └── input/\n   392\t│       └── InputBar.tsx\n   393\t├── pages/\n   394\t│   └── ChatPage.tsx\n   395\t├── types/\n   396\t│   └── chat.ts                    # Message, RiskCardPayload, ArtifactPayload interfaces\n   397\t├── lib/\n   398\t│   └── artifactSandbox.ts         # DOMPurify sanitiser + @babel/standalone JSX transpiler wrapper\n   399\t└── theme/\n   400\t    └── metrosense.ts              # MUI theme overrides\n   401\t```\n   402\t\n   403\t---\n   404\t\n   405\t## 10. Styling\n   406\t\n   407\t### 10.1 Colour Tokens\n   408\t\n   409\tAll values verified at WCAG 2.1 AA (text ≥ 4.5:1, UI components ≥ 3:1).\n   410\t\n   411\t| Token | Value | Usage |\n   412\t|-------|-------|-------|\n   413\t| `--bg-page` | `#F7F9FC` | Page / app background |\n   414\t| `--bg-surface` | `#FFFFFF` | Cards, bubbles, panels |\n   415\t| `--bg-sunken` | `#EEF2F7` | User bubble, input field |\n   416\t| `--border` | `#D1DBE8` | All dividers and borders |\n   417\t| `--border-strong` | `#C9D6E3` | Input fields, card outlines |\n   418\t| `--text-primary` | `#0F1F2E` | All body text (15.9:1 on `--bg-page`) |\n   419\t| `--text-secondary` | `#4A5568` | Labels, timestamps (7.1:1 on `--bg-page`) |\n   420\t| `--text-placeholder` | `#6B7B8D` | Input placeholder (4.6:1 — AA) |\n   421\t| `--accent` | `#005F8E` | Primary action, links, AI bubble border (6.6:1 on white) |\n   422\t| `--accent-hover` | `#004A70` | Hover/active state |\n   423\t| `--accent-text` | `#FFFFFF` | Text on `--accent` buttons (6.9:1) |\n   424\t| `--sev-critical-bg` | `#FFF0F2` | CRITICAL badge background |\n   425\t| `--sev-critical-text` | `#9B1D2A` | CRITICAL badge text (7.3:1) |\n   426\t| `--sev-critical-bar` | `#C0213A` | CRITICAL progress bar |\n   427\t| `--sev-high-bg` | `#FFF4ED` | HIGH badge background |\n   428\t| `--sev-high-text` | `#7C3005` | HIGH badge text (8.5:1) |\n   429\t| `--sev-high-bar` | `#C2520C` | HIGH progress bar |\n   430\t| `--sev-moderate-bg` | `#FFFBEB` | MODERATE badge background |\n   431\t| `--sev-moderate-text` | `#6B4C00` | MODERATE badge text (7.6:1) |\n   432\t| `--sev-moderate-bar` | `#B07D0A` | MODERATE progress bar |\n   433\t| `--sev-low-bg` | `#F0FDF4` | LOW badge background |\n   434\t| `--sev-low-text` | `#14532D` | LOW badge text (8.7:1) |\n   435\t| `--sev-low-bar` | `#16863E` | LOW progress bar |\n   436\t\n   437\t### 10.2 MUI Theme Override\n   438\t\n   439\t```typescript\n   440\t// theme/metrosense.ts\n   441\t{\n   442\t  palette: {\n   443\t    mode: 'light',\n   444\t    background: { default: '#F7F9FC', paper: '#FFFFFF' },\n   445\t    primary: { main: '#005F8E', contrastText: '#FFFFFF' },\n   446\t    error:   { main: '#C0213A' },\n   447\t    warning: { main: '#B07D0A' },\n   448\t    success: { main: '#16863E' },\n   449\t    text: { primary: '#0F1F2E', secondary: '#4A5568', disabled: '#6B7B8D' }\n   450\t  },\n   451\t  typography: {\n   452\t    fontFamily: \"'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif\",\n   453\t    fontSize: 14,\n   454\t    // Mono variant: sx={{ fontFamily: 'IBM Plex Mono, monospace' }}\n   455\t  },\n   456\t  components: {\n   457\t    MuiButtonBase: {\n   458\t      defaultProps: { disableRipple: false },\n   459\t      styleOverrides: {\n   460\t        root: {\n   461\t          '&:focus-visible': {\n   462\t            outline: '2px solid #005F8E',\n   463\t            outlineOffset: '2px',\n   464\t          }\n   465\t        }\n   466\t      }\n   467\t    }\n   468\t  }\n   469\t}\n   470\t```\n   471\t\n   472\t### 10.3 Accessibility Requirements\n   473\t\n   474\t- **Focus rings:** Every interactive element must show a visible `2px solid #005F8E` focus ring on keyboard focus. Never suppress `outline`.\n   475\t- **Colour independence:** Every status, severity, and state must also communicate via text label or icon — not colour alone.\n   476\t- **Touch targets:** All buttons and chips minimum `44 × 44px` tap target (WCAG 2.5.5).\n   477\t- **Motion:** Wrap all non-essential animations in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` — disable or reduce them.\n   478\t- **Semantic HTML:** Use `<main>`, `<header>`, `<section aria-label>` landmark roles. Message list: `role=\"log\" aria-live=\"polite\" aria-label=\"Conversation\"`. Streaming indicator: `role=\"status\" aria-live=\"polite\"`.\n   479\t- **Minimum font size:** 13px for any visible text. Secondary/timestamp labels may be 11px only if colour contrast ≥ 7:1.\n   480\t- **Link underlines:** Inline links in message text must be underlined (not colour-only).\n   481\t- **Custom scrollbar:** Thin, `#C9D6E3` track, `#005F8E` thumb. Ensure scrollbar thumb contrast ≥ 3:1 against track.\n   482\t\n   483\t---\n   484\t\n   485\t## 11. Environment Variables\n   486\t\n   487\tAdd to the scaffold's existing env var pattern:\n   488\t\n   489\t| Variable | Default | Description |\n   490\t|----------|---------|-------------|\n   491\t| `AGENT_SERVER_URL` | `http://agent:8020` | Internal Agent Server URL (Docker Compose service name). Only used if agent runs as a separate process; omit if in-process. |\n   492\t| `VITE_API_BASE_URL` | *(proxied via Vite → :8010)* | Frontend API base (already handled by Vite proxy in scaffold) |\n   493\t\n   494\t---\n   495\t\n   496\t## 12. Backend Route to Add\n   497\t\n   498\tAdd one route to `server/app/api/routes/`:\n   499\t\n   500\t```\n   501\tserver/app/api/routes/chat.py\n   502\t```\n   503\t\n   504\tResponsibilities:\n   505\t- Accept `POST /api/chat` with `{ session_id, message }`.\n   506\t- Call the Agent Server via `agent_proxy.stream_chat()`.\n   507\t- Stream the SSE response back to the client.\n   508\t- Accept `DELETE /api/chat/{session_id}` to clear agent session state.\n   509\t\n   510\tAdd one service:\n   511\t\n   512\t```\n   513\tserver/app/services/agent_proxy.py\n   514\t```\n   515\t\n   516\tResponsibilities:\n   517\t- `async def stream_chat(session_id, message) → AsyncIterator[str]`\n   518\t- **If Agent Server runs in-process:** import and call it directly — no HTTP overhead.\n   519\t- **If Agent Server runs as a separate process on localhost:** call `http://localhost:8020/chat` (internal only, never crosses the internet).\n   520\t- Handles timeout (30s), connection errors → emits `{\"type\": \"error\", ...}` on failure.\n   521\t\n   522\tAdd to `docker-compose.yml`:\n   523\t\n   524\t```yaml\n   525\tagent:\n   526\t  build: ./agent          # your Agent Server directory\n   527\t  ports:\n   528\t    - \"8020:8020\"         # internal only; not exposed if in-process\n   529\t  depends_on:\n   530\t    - db\n   531\t  environment:\n   532\t    - DATABASE_URL=${DATABASE_URL}\n   533\t```\n   534\t\n   535\t---\n   536\t\n   537\t## 13. Out of Scope (Not in this spec)\n   538\t\n   539\t- Authentication / user accounts.\n   540\t- Chat history persistence (no DB writes for chat messages).\n   541\t- Map rendering (separate concern — existing MetroSense dashboard).\n   542\t- Mobile-specific layout (responsive is sufficient — no native app).\n   543\t- Multi-agent routing or tool call visibility in the UI.\n   544\t- **Keyword-based or client-side RiskCard triggering.** The frontend must never inspect message content to decide whether to show a RiskCard. That decision belongs entirely to the agent.\n   545\t- **Artifact execution beyond the approved library whitelist.** The JSX artifact parser exposes only `React`, `ReactDOM`, `recharts`, `victory`, and `d3`. Any artifact requiring other libraries (e.g. Mapbox, Leaflet, Plotly) is out of scope for the inline renderer — the agent should not produce such artifacts. If it does, `ArtifactRenderer` must show the error state, not attempt execution.\n   546\t- **Agent-side artifact generation logic.** This spec covers only parsing and rendering on the frontend. How the agent decides to produce an artifact (and what chart library it uses) is defined in the agent spec, not here.\n   547\t- **Multi-artifact messages.** A single agent response renders at most one artifact. If the agent sends multiple `artifact` events in one response, only the last is rendered.\n   548\t\n   549\t---\n   550\t\n   551\t## 14. Implementation Order\n   552\t\n   553\t1. `theme/metrosense.ts` — get the design tokens right first.\n   554\t2. `Header.tsx` + `StatusPill` — static shell.\n   555\t3. `types/chat.ts` — lock the interfaces including `ArtifactPayload`.\n   556\t4. `useChat.ts` hook — SSE streaming logic (mock the backend with a local stub first); handle `artifact` event alongside `risk_card` and `token`.\n   557\t5. `InputBar.tsx` + `SuggestedPrompts.tsx`.\n   558\t6. `MessageBubble.tsx` + `ThinkingIndicator.tsx`.\n   559\t7. `RiskCard.tsx`.\n   560\t8. `lib/artifactSandbox.ts` — DOMPurify wrapper + `@babel/standalone` JSX transpiler; write unit tests for the sanitiser and the scope-limited evaluator before wiring into the UI.\n   561\t9. `hooks/useArtifactParser.ts` — async hook that takes an `ArtifactPayload` and returns `{ status: 'loading' | 'ready' | 'error', element: ReactNode | null, error?: string }`.\n   562\t10. `ArtifactRenderer.tsx` — compose the title bar, sandboxed content area, expand Modal, loading skeleton, and error state.\n   563\t11. `MessageList.tsx` — compose bubbles including `ArtifactRenderer`, auto-scroll logic.\n   564\t12. `ChatPage.tsx` — wire everything together.\n   565\t13. `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` + `agent_proxy.py` — real backend integration.\n"
```json
  }
}
```

Placeholder text `#6B7B8D` (contrast 4.6:1 — AA).\n   232\t- Visible `:focus` ring: `outline: 2px solid #005F8E`, `outline-offset: 2px` — never `outline: none`.\n   233\t- Placeholder text: `\"Ask about flood risk, AQI, traffic, power…\"`.\n   234\t- **Send button:** background `#005F8E`, icon + text label \"Send\" (visually hidden text for screen readers via `aria-label=\"Send message\"`). Disabled state: `#C9D6E3` background, `#6B7B8D` text, `aria-disabled=\"true\"`. `Enter` submits, `Shift+Enter` newline.\n   235\t- **Abort:** while streaming, button becomes \"Stop\" (square icon, `background: #DC2626`, `aria-label=\"Stop response\"`). Never rely on colour alone — icon + label both change.\n   236\t\n   237\t### 5.8 SuggestedPrompts\n   238\t\n   239\tShown only in the empty state (no messages yet). These reflect the UrbanClimate AI's core capabilities.\n   240\t\n   241\tFour chips in a 2×2 grid:\n   242\t- \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses should be barricaded?\"\n   243\t- \"How does current humidity compare to pre-monsoon averages of the last decade?\"\n   244\t- \"Which logistics routes through Bengaluru are highest risk today?\"\n   245\t- \"Generate a risk scorecard for Sarjapur Road.\"\n   246\t\n   247\t> **Note:** Only the last prompt reliably triggers a RiskCard — it explicitly requests a structured scorecard. The others are natural language queries that return streamed text. Chips are for user convenience, not a trigger mechanism.\n   248\t\n   249\tClicking a chip populates and submits the textarea.\n   250\t\n   251\t---\n   252\t\n   253\t## 6. API Contract\n   254\t\n   255\t### POST `/api/chat`\n   256\t\n   257\t**Request:**\n   258\t```json\n   259\t{\n   260\t  \"session_id\": \"uuid-v4\",\n   261\t  \"message\": \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses should be barricaded based on previous years?\"\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t**Response (streaming — `text/event-stream` SSE):**\n   266\t```\n   267\tdata: {\"type\": \"token\", \"content\": \"Based on the 2022 flood post-mortem\"}\n   268\tdata: {\"type\": \"token\", \"content\": \" and current drainage capacity...\"}\n   269\tdata: {\"type\": \"risk_card\", \"payload\": { ...structured risk JSON... }}\n   270\tdata: {\"type\": \"done\"}\n   271\t```\n   272\t\n   273\t**Error response:**\n   274\t```json\n   275\t{ \"error\": \"AGENT_UNAVAILABLE\", \"message\": \"...\" }\n   276\t```\n   277\t\n   278\tThe frontend handles five SSE event types:\n   279\t- `token` — append content to the current assistant bubble's text.\n   280\t- `risk_card` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `riskCard` and render a RiskCard below the streamed text. The frontend must never emit or synthesize this event itself. A single response can contain both `token` events and one `risk_card` event. The `risk_card` event may arrive before, during, or after the token stream — the frontend must handle all orderings gracefully.\n   281\t- `artifact` — **agent-emitted only.** Attach the payload to the current message as `artifact` and render an `ArtifactRenderer` below any RiskCard. Payload schema: `{ type: \"html\" | \"jsx\", title: string, source: string, description?: string }`. The frontend must sanitise/sandbox the content before rendering (see §5.5). A response may contain at most one `artifact` event; if multiple arrive, render only the last one and log a warning.\n   282\t- `done` — mark message complete, re-enable input.\n   283\t- `error` — show inline error banner in the message bubble.\n   284\t\n   285\t**GET `/api/health`** — used by StatusPill to poll agent connectivity (poll every 30s).\n   286\t\n   287\t---\n   288\t\n   289\t## 7. Risk Card JSON Schema\n   290\t\n   291\tThe agent emits this as a `risk_card` SSE event payload when it determines a structured neighborhood scorecard is the appropriate response. The frontend renders it as a RiskCard component. All top-level metric fields are optional — the agent only populates fields it has sufficient data to compute.\n   292\t\n   293\t```typescript\n   294\tinterface RiskCardPayload {\n   295\t  neighborhood: string;           // e.g. \"SARJAPUR ROAD\", \"BELLANDUR\", \"MANYATA TECH PARK\"\n   296\t  generated_at: string;           // ISO 8601 — timestamp of agent analysis\n   297\t  overall_risk_score: number;     // 0–10 composite score\n   298\t\n   299\t  // Cross-reference: DB rainfall forecast + drainage capacity docs + lake telemetry\n   300\t  flood_risk?: {\n   301\t    probability: number;          // 0–1\n   302\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   303\t  };\n   304\t\n   305\t  // Cross-reference: wind-speed telemetry + tree-density maps + historical outage logs\n   306\t  power_outage_risk?: {\n   307\t    probability: number;          // 0–1\n   308\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   309\t  };\n   310\t\n   311\t  // Derived from: rainfall threshold / traffic bottleneck historical correlation\n   312\t  traffic_delay_index?: {\n   313\t    congestion_score: number;     // 0–10\n   314\t    severity: \"CRITICAL\" | \"HIGH\" | \"MODERATE\" | \"LOW\";\n   315\t  };\n   316\t\n   317\t  // Derived from: AQI sensor data + Urban Heat Island research docs\n   318\t  health_advisory?: {\n   319\t    aqi: number;                  // raw AQI value\n   320\t    aqi_category: string;         // e.g. \"GOOD\", \"MODERATE\", \"UNHEALTHY FOR SENSITIVE GROUPS\", \"UNHEALTHY\", \"HAZARDOUS\"\n   321\t  };\n   322\t\n   323\t  // Agent's final recommendations based on full cross-analysis\n   324\t  emergency_readiness?: {\n   325\t    recommendation: string;       // e.g. \"Evacuate low-lying zones; barricade underpasses\"\n   326\t    actions?: string[];           // Specific action items, e.g. [\"Barricade Silk Board underpass\", \"Pre-position pumps at Bellandur inlet\"]\n   327\t  };\n   328\t}\n   329\t```\n   330\t\n   331\t---\n   332\t\n   333\t## 8. 412\t|-------|-------|-------|\n   413\t| `--bg-page` | `#F7F9FC` | Page / app background |\n   414\t| `--bg-surface` | `#FFFFFF` | Cards, bubbles, panels |\n   415\t| `--bg-sunken` | `#EEF2F7` | User bubble, input field |\n   416\t| `--border` | `#D1DBE8` | All dividers and borders |\n   417\t| `--border-strong` | `#C9D6E3` | Input fields, card outlines |\n   418\t| `--text-primary` | `#0F1F2E` | All body text (15.9:1 on `--bg-page`) |\n   419\t| `--text-secondary` | `#4A5568` | Labels, timestamps (7.1:1 on `--bg-page`) |\n   420\t| `--text-placeholder` | `#6B7B8D` | Input placeholder (4.6:1 — AA) |\n   421\t| `--accent` | `#005F8E` | Primary action, links, AI bubble border (6.6:1 on white) |\n   422\t| `--accent-hover` | `#004A70` | Hover/active state |\n   423\t| `--accent-text` | `#FFFFFF` | Text on `--accent` buttons (6.9:1) |\n   424\t| `--sev-critical-bg` | `#FFF0F2` | CRITICAL badge background |\n   425\t| `--sev-critical-text` | `#9B1D2A` | CRITICAL badge text (7.3:1) |\n   426\t| `--sev-critical-bar` | `#C0213A` | CRITICAL progress bar |\n   427\t| `--sev-high-bg` | `#FFF4ED` | HIGH badge background |\n   428\t| `--sev-high-text` | `#7C3005` | HIGH badge text (8.5:1) |\n   429\t| `--sev-high-bar` | `#C2520C` | HIGH progress bar |\n   430\t| `--sev-moderate-bg` | `#FFFBEB` | MODERATE badge background |\n   431\t| `--sev-moderate-text` | `#6B4C00` | MODERATE badge text (7.6:1) |\n   432\t| `--sev-moderate-bar` | `#B07D0A` | MODERATE progress bar |\n   433\t| `--sev-low-bg` | `#F0FDF4` | LOW badge background |\n   434\t| `--sev-low-text` | `#14532D` | LOW badge text (8.7:1) |\n   435\t| `--sev-low-bar` | `#16863E` | LOW progress bar |\n   436\t\n   437\t### 10.2 MUI Theme Override\n   438\t\n   439\t```typescript\n   440\t// theme/metrosense.ts\n   441\t{\n   442\t  palette: {\n   443\t    mode: 'light',\n   444\t    background: { default: '#F7F9FC', paper: '#FFFFFF' },\n   445\t    primary: { main: '#005F8E', contrastText: '#FFFFFF' },\n   446\t    error:   { main: '#C0213A' },\n   447\t    warning: { main: '#B07D0A' },\n   448\t    success: { main: '#16863E' },\n   449\t    text: { primary: '#0F1F2E', secondary: '#4A5568', disabled: '#6B7B8D' }\n   450\t  },\n   451\t  typography: {\n   452\t    fontFamily: \"'IBM Plex Sans', sans-serif\",\n   453\t    fontSize: 14,\n   454\t    // Mono variant: sx={{ fontFamily: 'IBM Plex Mono, monospace' }}\n   455\t  },\n   456\t  components: {\n   457\t    MuiButtonBase: {\n   458\t      defaultProps: { disableRipple: false },\n   459\t      styleOverrides: {\n   460\t        root: {\n   461\t          '&:focus-visible': {\n   462\t            outline: '2px solid #005F8E',\n   463\t            outlineOffset: '2px',\n   464\t          }\n   465\t        }\n   466\t      }\n   467\t    }\n   468\t  }\n   469\t}\n   470\t```\n   471\t\n   472\t### 10.3 Accessibility Requirements\n   473\t\n   474\t- **Focus rings:** Every interactive element must show a visible `2px solid #005F8E` focus ring on keyboard focus. Never suppress `outline`.\n   475\t- **Colour independence:** Every status, severity, and state must also communicate via text label or icon — not colour alone.\n   476\t- **Touch targets:** All buttons and chips minimum `44 × 44px` tap target (WCAG 2.5.5).\n   477\t- **Motion:** Wrap all non-essential animations in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` — disable or reduce them.\n   478\t- **Semantic HTML:** Use `<main>`, `<header>`, `<section aria-label>` landmark roles. Message list: `role=\"log\" aria-live=\"polite\" aria-label=\"Conversation\"`. Streaming indicator: `role=\"status\" aria-live=\"polite\"`.\n   479\t- **Minimum font size:** 13px for any visible text. Secondary/timestamp labels may be 11px only if colour contrast ≥ 7:1.\n   480\t- **Link underlines:** Inline links in message text must be underlined (not colour-only).\n   481\t- **Custom scrollbar:** Thin, `#C9D6E3` track, `#005F8E` thumb. Ensure scrollbar thumb contrast ≥ 3:1 against track.\n   482\t\n   483\t---\n   484\t\n   485\t## 11. Environment Variables\n   486\t\n   487\tAdd to the scaffold's existing env var pattern:\n   488\t\n   489\t| Variable | Default | Description |\n   490\t|----------|---------|-------------|\n   491\t| `AGENT_SERVER_URL` | `http://agent:8020` | Internal Agent Server URL (Docker Compose service name). Only used if agent runs as a separate process; omit if in-process. |\n   492\t| `VITE_API_BASE_URL` | *(proxied via Vite → :8010)* | Frontend API base (already handled by Vite proxy in scaffold) |\n   493\t\n   494\t---\n   495\t\n   496\t## 12. Backend Route to Add\n   497\t\n   498\tAdd one route to `server/app/api/routes/`:\n   499\t\n   500\t```\n   501\tserver/app/api/routes/chat.py\n   502\t```\n   503\t\n   504\tResponsibilities:\n   505\t- Accept `POST /api/chat` with `{ session_id, message }`.\n   506\t- Call the Agent Server via `agent_proxy.stream_chat()`.\n   507\t- Stream the SSE response back to the client.\n   508\t- Accept `DELETE /api/chat/{session_id}` to clear agent session state.\n   509\t\n   510\tAdd one service:\n   511\t\n   512\t```\n   513\tserver/app/services/agent_proxy.py\n   514\t```\n   515\t\n   516\tResponsibilities:\n   517\t- `async def stream_chat(session_id, message) → AsyncIterator[str]`\n   518\t- **If Agent Server runs in-process:** import and call it directly — no HTTP overhead.\n   519\t- **If Agent Server runs as a separate process on localhost:** call `http://localhost:8020/chat` (internal only, never crosses the internet).\n   520\t- Handles timeout (30s), connection errors → emits `{\"type\": \"error\", ...}` on failure.\n   521\t\n   522\tAdd to `docker-compose.yml`:\n   523\t\n   524\t```yaml\n   525\tagent:\n   526\t  build: ./agent          # your Agent Server directory\n   527\t  ports:\n   528\t    - \"8020:8020\"         # internal only; not exposed if in-process\n   529\t  depends_on:\n   530\t    - db\n   531\t  environment:\n   532\t    - DATABASE_URL=${DATABASE_URL}\n   533\t```\n   534\t\n   535\t---\n   536\t\n   537\t## 13. Out of Scope (Not in this spec)\n   538\t\n   539\t- Authentication / user accounts.\n   540\t- Chat history persistence (no DB writes for chat messages).\n   541\t- Map rendering (separate concern — existing MetroSense dashboard).\n   542\t- Mobile-specific layout (responsive is sufficient — no native app).\n   543\t- Multi-agent routing or tool call visibility in the UI.\n   544\t- **Keyword-based or client-side RiskCard triggering.** The frontend must never inspect message content to decide whether to show a RiskCard. That decision belongs entirely to the agent.\n   545\t- **Artifact execution beyond the approved library whitelist.** The JSX artifact parser exposes only `React`, `ReactDOM`, `recharts`, `victory`, and `d3`. Any artifact requiring other libraries (e.g. Mapbox, Leaflet, Plotly) is out of scope for the inline renderer — the agent should not produce such artifacts. If it does, `ArtifactRenderer` must show the error state, not attempt execution.\n   546\t- **Agent-side artifact generation logic.** This spec covers only parsing and rendering on the frontend. How the agent decides to produce an artifact (and what chart library it uses) is defined in the agent spec, not here.\n   547\t- **Multi-artifact messages.** A single agent response renders at most one artifact. If the agent sends multiple `artifact` events in one response, only the last is rendered.\n   548\t\n   549\t---\n   550\t\n   551\t## 14. Implementation Order\n   552\t\n   553\t1. `theme/metrosense.ts` — get the design tokens right first.\n   554\t2. `Header.tsx` + `StatusPill` — static shell.\n   555\t3. `types/chat.ts` — lock the interfaces including `ArtifactPayload`.\n   556\t4. `useChat.ts` hook — SSE streaming logic (mock the backend with a local stub first); handle `artifact` event alongside `risk_card` and `token`.\n   557\t5. `InputBar.tsx` + `SuggestedPrompts.tsx`.\n   558\t6. `MessageBubble.tsx` + `ThinkingIndicator.tsx`.\n   559\t7. `RiskCard.tsx`.\n   560\t8. `lib/artifactSandbox.ts` — DOMPurify wrapper + `@babel/standalone` JSX transpiler; write unit tests for the sanitiser and the scope-limited evaluator before wiring into the UI.\n   561\t9. `hooks/useArtifactParser.ts` — async hook that takes an `ArtifactPayload` and returns `{ status: 'loading' | 'ready' | 'error', element: ReactNode | null, error?: string }`.\n   562\t10. `ArtifactRenderer.tsx` — compose the title bar, sandboxed content area, expand Modal, loading skeleton, and error state.\n   563\t11. `MessageList.tsx` — compose bubbles including `ArtifactRenderer`, auto-scroll logic.\n   564\t12. `ChatPage.tsx` — wire everything together.\n   565\t13. `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` + `agent_proxy.py` — real backend integration.\n"
```json
  }
}
```

## Entry 172
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:30:08.030Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:30:08.030Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "A few important issues stood out. I’d address these before treating `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` as implementation-ready.\n\n**Findings**\n\n1. `allow-scripts` + `allow-same-origin` on `srcdoc` artifacts weakens the sandbox too much for untrusted agent output.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:175`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:192`  \n   The spec says agent-produced HTML is sanitized, then rendered in `<iframe srcdoc ... sandbox=\"allow-scripts allow-same-origin\" />`. For untrusted content, that combination is the risky one: you’re allowing script execution and same-origin behavior at the same time. If artifacts are truly agent-generated, this is the sharpest security edge in the whole design.  \n   Recommended change: prefer one of these:\n   - `html` artifacts: sanitize and render with a stricter sandbox, ideally without `allow-same-origin`\n   - `jsx` artifacts: don’t use `new Function` in the main app runtime at all; either move rendering to a dedicated isolated frame/worker-like boundary, or reduce v1 to sanitized HTML/table/chart JSON instead of executable JSX\n\n2. The streaming contract is underspecified for browser implementation, and the current client scaffold does not support the proposed POST-SSE shape directly.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:255`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:283`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:376`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:380`, `client/src/lib/api.ts:1`, `client/vite.config.ts:8`  \n   The spec uses `POST /api/chat` returning `text/event-stream`, but doesn’t define how the frontend reads it. Native `EventSource` does not support POST, and the current client only has Axios configured. That means the frontend will need a `fetch()`-based stream reader with manual SSE parsing, abort handling, partial-chunk buffering, and error semantics.  \n   Recommended change: lock this down in the spec:\n   - say explicitly that chat streaming uses `fetch` + `ReadableStream`, not `EventSource`\n   - define newline framing, malformed event behavior, and abort behavior\n   - define whether the backend sends heartbeat events to keep proxies from closing the stream\n\n3. Health/status semantics are inconsistent between the spec and the current backend, and the status pill may show false health.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:81`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:84`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:285`, `client/vite.config.ts:8`–`client/vite.config.ts:10`, `server/app/api/routes/health.py:1`  \n   The spec says `GET /api/health` is used to poll “agent connectivity”, but the repo currently exposes `GET /health`, and that route only reports backend health. Those are different things. A green pill that only proves FastAPI is alive is misleading if the agent server is down.  \n   Recommended change: split this explicitly:\n   - `GET /health`: backend liveness\n   - `GET /api/health` or `GET /api/status`: composite backend+agent readiness used by the UI\n   - define the response shape, e.g. backend status, agent status, degraded reason\n\n4. The spec mixes two mutually exclusive agent integration models without choosing one for v1.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:38`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:491`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:517`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:520`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:524`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:533`  \n   It says the backend may call the agent in-process or over localhost, but then also defines `AGENT_SERVER_URL`, Docker Compose wiring, and an `agent` service. That leaves an implementer making architecture decisions mid-build.  \n   Recommended change: pick one for v1.\n   - Best for your current direction: separate `agents/` service over internal HTTP\n   - Then make the in-process path explicitly “future optimization” or remove it from this spec\n\n5. The artifact approach is too heavy for a first frontend iteration and drags large security/performance complexity into the UI spec.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:150`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:218`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:560`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:562`  \n   Supporting DOMPurify, `@babel/standalone`, runtime JSX transpilation, controlled evaluation, chart-library whitelisting, iframe autosizing, modal reuse, and error boundaries is a lot for v1 chat. It’s buildable, but it’s not “minimal” anymore.  \n   Recommended design change: narrow v1 artifacts to one of:\n   - sanitized HTML only\n   - structured chart JSON rendered by frontend-owned components\n   - plain tables/cards only, defer executable JSX artifacts to v2\n\n**Lower-severity issues**\n\n6. The spec has naming/structure drift that will cause implementation confusion.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:31`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:370`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:400`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:524`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:526`  \n   It refers to `agent/` in some places, while your current direction is `agents/`. It also lists `lib/` twice in the file tree.  \n   Recommended change: normalize on `agents/` everywhere and clean up the file tree.\n\n**What I’d change in the spec**\n1. Pick one agent topology for v1: `client -> server -> agents` over internal HTTP.\n2. Replace “POST SSE” ambiguity with an explicit `fetch` streaming contract.\n3. Separate backend liveness from agent readiness in the health model.\n4. Reduce artifact v1 scope to sanitized HTML or structured chart data.\n5. Normalize naming to `agents/`.\n\n**Residual risks / test gaps**\n- No frontend test plan is defined yet for stream parsing, out-of-order `risk_card`/`artifact` events, or abort/retry behavior.\n- No security test cases are defined for malicious artifact payloads.\n- No contract tests are defined for backend-to-agent streaming and composite health reporting.\n\nIf you want, I can rewrite `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` into a tighter v1-ready spec with those decisions locked down.",
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
        "text": "A few important issues stood out. I’d address these before treating `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` as implementation-ready.\n\n**Findings**\n\n1. `allow-scripts` + `allow-same-origin` on `srcdoc` artifacts weakens the sandbox too much for untrusted agent output.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:175`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:192`  \n   The spec says agent-produced HTML is sanitized, then rendered in `<iframe srcdoc ... sandbox=\"allow-scripts allow-same-origin\" />`. For untrusted content, that combination is the risky one: you’re allowing script execution and same-origin behavior at the same time. If artifacts are truly agent-generated, this is the sharpest security edge in the whole design.  \n   Recommended change: prefer one of these:\n   - `html` artifacts: sanitize and render with a stricter sandbox, ideally without `allow-same-origin`\n   - `jsx` artifacts: don’t use `new Function` in the main app runtime at all; either move rendering to a dedicated isolated frame/worker-like boundary, or reduce v1 to sanitized HTML/table/chart JSON instead of executable JSX\n\n2. The streaming contract is underspecified for browser implementation, and the current client scaffold does not support the proposed POST-SSE shape directly.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:255`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:283`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:376`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:380`, `client/src/lib/api.ts:1`, `client/vite.config.ts:8`  \n   The spec uses `POST /api/chat` returning `text/event-stream`, but doesn’t define how the frontend reads it. Native `EventSource` does not support POST, and the current client only has Axios configured. That means the frontend will need a `fetch()`-based stream reader with manual SSE parsing, abort handling, partial-chunk buffering, and error semantics.  \n   Recommended change: lock this down in the spec:\n   - say explicitly that chat streaming uses `fetch` + `ReadableStream`, not `EventSource`\n   - define newline framing, malformed event behavior, and abort behavior\n   - define whether the backend sends heartbeat events to keep proxies from closing the stream\n\n3. Health/status semantics are inconsistent between the spec and the current backend, and the status pill may show false health.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:81`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:84`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:285`, `client/vite.config.ts:8`–`client/vite.config.ts:10`, `server/app/api/routes/health.py:1`  \n   The spec says `GET /api/health` is used to poll “agent connectivity”, but the repo currently exposes `GET /health`, and that route only reports backend health. Those are different things. A green pill that only proves FastAPI is alive is misleading if the agent server is down.  \n   Recommended change: split this explicitly:\n   - `GET /health`: backend liveness\n   - `GET /api/health` or `GET /api/status`: composite backend+agent readiness used by the UI\n   - define the response shape, e.g. backend status, agent status, degraded reason\n\n4. The spec mixes two mutually exclusive agent integration models without choosing one for v1.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:38`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:491`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:517`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:520`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:524`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:533`  \n   It says the backend may call the agent in-process or over localhost, but then also defines `AGENT_SERVER_URL`, Docker Compose wiring, and an `agent` service. That leaves an implementer making architecture decisions mid-build.  \n   Recommended change: pick one for v1.\n   - Best for your current direction: separate `agents/` service over internal HTTP\n   - Then make the in-process path explicitly “future optimization” or remove it from this spec\n\n5. The artifact approach is too heavy for a first frontend iteration and drags large security/performance complexity into the UI spec.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:150`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:218`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:560`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:562`  \n   Supporting DOMPurify, `@babel/standalone`, runtime JSX transpilation, controlled evaluation, chart-library whitelisting, iframe autosizing, modal reuse, and error boundaries is a lot for v1 chat. It’s buildable, but it’s not “minimal” anymore.  \n   Recommended design change: narrow v1 artifacts to one of:\n   - sanitized HTML only\n   - structured chart JSON rendered by frontend-owned components\n   - plain tables/cards only, defer executable JSX artifacts to v2\n\n**Lower-severity issues**\n\n6. The spec has naming/structure drift that will cause implementation confusion.  \n   `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:31`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:370`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:400`, `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:524`–`docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:526`  \n   It refers to `agent/` in some places, while your current direction is `agents/`. It also lists `lib/` twice in the file tree.  \n   Recommended change: normalize on `agents/` everywhere and clean up the file tree.\n\n**What I’d change in the spec**\n1. Pick one agent topology for v1: `client -> server -> agents` over internal HTTP.\n2. Replace “POST SSE” ambiguity with an explicit `fetch` streaming contract.\n3. Separate backend liveness from agent readiness in the health model.\n4. Reduce artifact v1 scope to sanitized HTML or structured chart data.\n5. Normalize naming to `agents/`.\n\n**Residual risks / test gaps**\n- No frontend test plan is defined yet for stream parsing, out-of-order `risk_card`/`artifact` events, or abort/retry behavior.\n- No security test cases are defined for malicious artifact payloads.\n- No contract tests are defined for backend-to-agent streaming and composite health reporting.\n\nIf you want, I can rewrite `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` into a tighter v1-ready spec with those decisions locked down."
      }
    ],
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
  "type": "turn_context",
  "payload": {
    "turn_id": "019cd71d-b3d9-7c50-b3dd-054b61cf1f00",
    "cwd": "/home/dell/ai-track-metrosense",
    "current_date": "2026-03-10",
    "timezone": "Asia/Kolkata",
    "approval_policy": "on-request",
    "sandbox_policy": {
      "type": "workspace-write",
      "writable_roots": [
        "/home/dell/.codex/memories"
      ],
      "network_access": false,
      "exclude_tmpdir_env_var": false,
      "exclude_slash_tmp": false
    },
    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "plan",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.4",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

## Entry 181
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:39:54.889Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:39:54.889Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "Yes — that is a much better v1.\n\nIf you are dropping SSE for now and limiting artifact output to sanitized HTML only, the design becomes much safer and much easier to implement.\n\nWhat I’d lock in next:\n\n**Good decisions**\n- No SSE for v1\n- `client -> server -> agents`\n- `agents/` as the canonical folder/service name\n- Composite backend+agent health status\n- HTML-only artifacts from the agent, no runtime JSX execution\n\n**One important follow-up**\nYou should replace the current chat contract with a simple non-streaming response shape.\n\nRecommended v1 contract:\n- `POST /api/chat`\n- Request: `{ session_id, message }`\n- Response:\n```json\n{\n  \"message\": \"text answer from the agent\",\n  \"artifact_html\": \"<div>...</div>\",\n  \"risk_card\": null,\n  \"error\": null\n}\n```\n\nOr, if you want clearer separation:\n```json\n{\n  \"message\": \"text answer from the agent\",\n  \"artifact\": {\n    \"type\": \"html\",\n    \"title\": \"Rainfall Trend - Sarjapur Road\",\n    \"source\": \"<div>...</div>\",\n    \"description\": \"7-day rainfall trend\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThat avoids future confusion from SSE language still being present in the spec.\n\n**A few more things to address**\n\n1. `HTML sanitization policy`\n- Define exactly what is allowed:\n  - inline SVG allowed or not\n  - inline styles allowed or not\n  - tables allowed\n  - iframes disallowed\n  - scripts disallowed\n  - external URLs disallowed\n- This should be explicit in the spec.\n\n2. `Artifact rendering boundary`\n- Even with HTML-only artifacts, render them in a sandboxed iframe rather than injecting directly into the app DOM.\n- That keeps layout/style bleed and script surprises contained.\n\n3. `Health endpoint shape`\n- Define the exact JSON.\nExample:\n```json\n{\n  \"backend\": \"ok\",\n  \"agent\": \"ok\",\n  \"status\": \"online\"\n}\n```\nOr degraded:\n```json\n{\n  \"backend\": \"ok\",\n  \"agent\": \"down\",\n  \"status\": \"degraded\"\n}\n```\n\n4. `Failure behavior`\n- Define what the frontend does when:\n  - backend is up, agent is down\n  - chat request times out\n  - returned HTML is invalid or stripped by sanitizer\n- This prevents UI ambiguity later.\n\n5. `Testing`\nFor v1, you should explicitly require tests for:\n- sanitizer strips scripts/event handlers\n- artifact iframe renders safe HTML\n- `/api/chat` returns plain text only\n- `/api/chat` returns text + artifact HTML\n- degraded health state shows correctly\n\n6. `Spec cleanup`\n- Remove SSE/event-type language entirely\n- Remove JSX/Babel/recharts/victory runtime execution sections\n- Rename `agent/` to `agents/`\n- Update implementation order to reflect non-streaming chat\n\nSo overall: yes, this is now a solid v1 direction.\n\nThe main remaining thing is to make the spec internally consistent with:\n- non-streaming chat\n- HTML-only artifact rendering\n- internal `agents/` service\n- explicit health and error contracts\n\nIf you want, I can rewrite the relevant sections of `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` into a cleaner v1 spec.",
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
    "last_agent_message": "Yes — that is a much better v1.\n\nIf you are dropping SSE for now and limiting artifact output to sanitized HTML only, the design becomes much safer and much easier to implement.\n\nWhat I’d lock in next:\n\n**Good decisions**\n- No SSE for v1\n- `client -> server -> agents`\n- `agents/` as the canonical folder/service name\n- Composite backend+agent health status\n- HTML-only artifacts from the agent, no runtime JSX execution\n\n**One important follow-up**\nYou should replace the current chat contract with a simple non-streaming response shape.\n\nRecommended v1 contract:\n- `POST /api/chat`\n- Request: `{ session_id, message }`\n- Response:\n```json\n{\n  \"message\": \"text answer from the agent\",\n  \"artifact_html\": \"<div>...</div>\",\n  \"risk_card\": null,\n  \"error\": null\n}\n```\n\nOr, if you want clearer separation:\n```json\n{\n  \"message\": \"text answer from the agent\",\n  \"artifact\": {\n    \"type\": \"html\",\n    \"title\": \"Rainfall Trend - Sarjapur Road\",\n    \"source\": \"<div>...</div>\",\n    \"description\": \"7-day rainfall trend\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThat avoids future confusion from SSE language still being present in the spec.\n\n**A few more things to address**\n\n1. `HTML sanitization policy`\n- Define exactly what is allowed:\n  - inline SVG allowed or not\n  - inline styles allowed or not\n  - tables allowed\n  - iframes disallowed\n  - scripts disallowed\n  - external URLs disallowed\n- This should be explicit in the spec.\n\n2. `Artifact rendering boundary`\n- Even with HTML-only artifacts, render them in a sandboxed iframe rather than injecting directly into the app DOM.\n- That keeps layout/style bleed and script surprises contained.\n\n3. `Health endpoint shape`\n- Define the exact JSON.\nExample:\n```json\n{\n  \"backend\": \"ok\",\n  \"agent\": \"ok\",\n  \"status\": \"online\"\n}\n```\nOr degraded:\n```json\n{\n  \"backend\": \"ok\",\n  \"agent\": \"down\",\n  \"status\": \"degraded\"\n}\n```\n\n4. `Failure behavior`\n- Define what the frontend does when:\n  - backend is up, agent is down\n  - chat request times out\n  - returned HTML is invalid or stripped by sanitizer\n- This prevents UI ambiguity later.\n\n5. `Testing`\nFor v1, you should explicitly require tests for:\n- sanitizer strips scripts/event handlers\n- artifact iframe renders safe HTML\n- `/api/chat` returns plain text only\n- `/api/chat` returns text + artifact HTML\n- degraded health state shows correctly\n\n6. `Spec cleanup`\n- Remove SSE/event-type language entirely\n- Remove JSX/Babel/recharts/victory runtime execution sections\n- Rename `agent/` to `agents/`\n- Update implementation order to reflect non-streaming chat\n\nSo overall: yes, this is now a solid v1 direction.\n\nThe main remaining thing is to make the spec internally consistent with:\n- non-streaming chat\n- HTML-only artifact rendering\n- internal `agents/` service\n- explicit health and error contracts\n\nIf you want, I can rewrite the relevant sections of `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md` into a cleaner v1 spec."
  }
}
```

## Entry 186
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T09:40:54.071Z
**Role/Type:** turn_context
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T09:40:54.071Z",
  "type": "turn_context",
  "payload": {
    "turn_id": "019cd71e-ecb6-72c1-80d2-cb893ca38c32",
    "cwd": "/home/dell/ai-track-metrosense",
    "current_date": "2026-03-10",
    "timezone": "Asia/Kolkata",
    "approval_policy": "on-request",
    "sandbox_policy": {
      "type": "workspace-write",
      "writable_roots": [
        "/home/dell/.codex/memories"
      ],
      "network_access": false,
      "exclude_tmpdir_env_var": false,
      "exclude_slash_tmp": false
    },
    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "plan",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.4",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

```json
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```
